The magic of perspective (nonlinear) interpolation in opengl shaders comes from the fourth coordinate, W. (As in position.xyzw.) Default "no-op" value is w= 1.0.

The Z coordinate is thrown away (or clipped -1 to +1 against). But xyz are all divided by W on their way to screen space.

So, interpolating linearly on W, then dividing by it, gives perspective, straight lines remain straight, &c.

I hope you won't be offended if I suggest you're approaching the topic from the wrong end. Focusing on "micro-differences" is rarely productive, and is a subject best left to the compiler. Significant performance improvements are found by looking at 1) algorithms, 2) parallelism, 3) use of optimized libraries (for example, Intel MKL) instead of rolling your own. Especially when you get into I/O, very little is under your control. Sure, formatted I/O with formats in variables is less efficient than compile-time formats, unformatted is faster still. @liminal: If your object (Bar) is not scoped (e.g. `@Singleton`), then **getting it won't change the object graph**; across all consumers, direct injection of Bar will create an instance, `Provider<Bar>` will return a new instance each time you call `get`, and `Lazy<Bar>` will generate a new instance on `get` and always return the same one. If your object is scoped, then calling `get` will cause the object to be created for everyone, but no differently than if you had injected it aside from the delay. (For a scoped object, Provider and Lazy are equivalent.)

If you use Flink's exactly once, you will get the desired behavior for free: data is written inside a transaction to the Kafka topics and only committed once a checkpoint has been succeeded. Thus on failure and recovery, only data is re-processed which has not produced a committed output yet. Of course, data may end up several times in the `dead` topic, if the API failure persists over several retries, but it won't be processed more than once at a time.

Rounding numbers inside the program does make sense if you lose precision and need to compare the result to something. Example, if you multiply a matrix (internally a double array) to its inverted you should get the identity matrix, but as you can lose precision inside the multiplication routine, you can get small numbers in places you should get zeroes. If you try to compare the result to a real identity matrix, you'll get a FALSE instead an expected TRUE. So rounding the multiplication result can be a good idea in this case. My way to round: `#define ROUND(n,p) round(n*pow(10,p))/pow(10,p)`

Rather, removing the parentheses uses a special syntax feature called operator sections. `(==) Red` is equivalent to `(Red ==)` (a left-section). `(== Red)` is equivalent to `\x -> x == Red` or `flip (==) Red`. To go from `(==) Red` to `(== Red)` directly we have to realise that `==` is symmetric. If you do that, then `((==) Red)` becomes `((== Red))` and then clearly a set of parentheses is redundant.

In Fortran, the leftmost array subscript varies fastest, yet your loops use the outermost loop for that index. This is a common mistake made by people who don't know Fortran well. Some compilers will rearrange the loops on their own, but you can help by doing so yourself.
